english:
Regular verbs form the Partizip II with ge- + stem + -t:
machen → gemacht, recyceln → recycelt

Irregular verbs use ge- + stem change + -en:
lesen → gelesen, brechen → gebrochen

Verbs with inseparable prefixes (ver-, be-, er-, zer-) skip ge-:
verschmutzen → verschmutzt, zerstören → zerstört

Verbs ending in -ieren also skip ge-:
recyceln → recycelt, produzieren → produziert

As adjectives, all forms decline normally:
der verschmutzte Fluss (nominative)
den verschmutzten Fluss (accusative)
dem verschmutzten Fluss (dative)

German packs modifiers BEFORE the noun, framed between the article and the noun itself.

- die [in Berlin lebende] Frau (the woman living in Berlin)
- das [von Goethe geschriebene] Buch (the book written by Goethe)
- der [seit Wochen geplante] Urlaub (the vacation planned for weeks)

How to read them: find the article, jump to the NOUN, then read everything in between.
How to build them: start with a relative clause, remove the relative pronoun, and place the participle with its ending right before the noun.

English: 'The woman who lives in Berlin'
Formal German: 'Die in Berlin lebende Frau'

Complex information in a single noun phrase. Standard in academic writing.
Extreme examples exist in legal texts.
Start simple and build up.

Practice converting:
'Der Fluss, der verschmutzt ist' = 'Der verschmutzte Fluss'
'Das Papier, das recycelt wurde' = 'Das recycelte Papier'
'Die Frau, die in Berlin lebt' = 'Die in Berlin lebende Frau'

Formation: sein + Partizip II
Present: ist geöffnet (is open)
Past: war geöffnet (was open)

Focus: the STATE or RESULT, not the process.

Process passive (werden): Das Fenster wird geöffnet.
(Someone is opening it right now.)

State passive (sein): Das Fenster ist geöffnet.
(It is in an open state.)

Many state passive forms look like adjective + sein:
Das Geschäft ist geschlossen.
Der Kaffee ist gekocht.
Ist der Kaffee fertig?

The boundary between state passive and adjective is fluid in daily life.
For the exam: focus on the difference werden (process) vs. sein (state).

Standard passive: Das Buch wird dem Kind geschenkt. (The book is given to the child.)
bekommen passive: Das Kind bekommt das Buch geschenkt. (The child gets the book given.)

The RECIPIENT becomes the subject.

Very common in spoken German.
kriegen works identically: Das Kind kriegt das Buch geschenkt.

Use it when you want to emphasize the RECIPIENT.
Natural with verbs of giving, sending, and explaining.

Standard: Mir wurde geholfen. (I was helped.)
bekommen: Ich bekam Hilfe. (I received help.)

Some grammarians call it colloquial, but it is standard spoken German.

The construction sein plus zu plus infinitive has TWO meanings depending on context:
- Possibility: Das ist zu schaffen. (That can be done.)
- Necessity: Die Regeln sind zu beachten. (The rules must be followed.)

The suffix -bar works like English -able or -ible:
- machbar (doable), lösbar (solvable), essbar (edible)

Adding un- negates the meaning: unlösbar, unvorstellbar, unessbar.
Irregular vowel change: a → ä in du/er/sie/es. Past: ließ. Partizip II: gelassen (or lassen with another infinitive).

Lassen has three different meanings:

1. TO HAVE DONE: etwas machen lassen
'Ich lasse mein Auto reparieren.' (I have my car repaired.)
'Sie lässt sich die Haare schneiden.' (She has her hair cut.)

2. TO ALLOW: jemanden etwas tun lassen
'Lass mich erklären!' (Let me explain!)
'Er lässt sie gehen.' (He lets her go.)

3. TO LEAVE/STOP: aufhören, zurücklassen
'Lass das!' (Stop that!)
'Ich lasse den Schirm hier.' (I leave the umbrella here.)

Passive alternative: sich lassen = can be done
'Das lässt sich machen.' (That can be done.)

In the Perfekt, lassen has two Partizip II forms:

1. As a main verb: gelassen
'Ich habe den Hund zu Hause gelassen.' (I left the dog at home.)

2. With another infinitive: lassen (double infinitive)
'Ich habe mein Auto reparieren lassen.' (I had my car repaired.)

The double infinitive construction (two infinitives at the end) is standard in German. It also applies to modal verbs in Perfekt:
'Ich habe arbeiten müssen.' (I had to work.)
Wasch (wash) + Mittel (means/agent). Germany has strict\nWaschmittel regulations. Phosphate bans started here in the 1980s.
Wasch (wash) + Mittel (means/agent). Detergent. 'Ökologisches Waschmittel' (ecological detergent). Germany has strict Waschmittel regulations. Phosphate bans started here in the 1980s.
From French 'batterie.' Germany collects used Batterien\nin special green boxes at every supermarket.
From French 'batterie.' 'Batterie einlegen' (to insert a battery). 'Aufladbare Batterie' (rechargeable battery). Germany collects used Batterien in special green boxes at every supermarket.
Feuer (fire) + Zeug (stuff/tool). Fire-tool.\n'Hast du ein Feuerzeug?' is a classic conversation starter.
Feuer (fire) + Zeug (stuff/tool). Fire-tool. A lighter. 'Hast du ein Feuerzeug?' is a classic conversation starter. Das Feuerzeug, neuter, like most -zeug compounds.
Streich (stroke) + Holz (wood). Strike-wood.\nMore poetic than Feuerzeug: a stick you stroke into flame.
Streich (stroke) + Holz (wood). Strike-wood. A match. 'Streichhölzer anzünden' (to light matches). More poetic than Feuerzeug. The image: a stick of wood you stroke into flame.
'Beißzange' (cutting pliers), 'Zuckerzange' (sugar tongs).\n'Jemanden in die Zange nehmen' means to put the squeeze on someone.
'Beißzange' (cutting pliers), 'Zuckerzange' (sugar tongs). 'Jemanden in die Zange nehmen' (to put the squeeze on someone). A gripping tool for work and idioms.
'Wasserrohr' (water pipe), 'Abflussrohr' (drain pipe).\nAlso means 'reed' (the plant). Plumbing runs on Rohre.
'Wasserrohr' (water pipe), 'Abflussrohr' (drain pipe). Also means 'reed' (the plant). 'Rohrleitung' (pipeline). 'Durch die Rohre' means through the pipes. Plumbing runs on Rohre.
Same as English. 'Sandstrand' (sandy beach).\n'Den Kopf in den Sand stecken' means to bury one's head in the sand.
Same word as English. 'Sandstrand' (sandy beach), 'Sandkasten' (sandbox). 'Den Kopf in den Sand stecken' (to bury one's head in the sand) works identically in both languages.
Cognate with English 'stone.' 'Edelstein' (gemstone).\n'Ein Stein vom Herzen' (relief, literally: a stone off the heart).
Cognate with English 'stone.' 'Ziegelstein' (brick), 'Edelstein' (gemstone). 'Ein Stein vom Herzen' (a stone off the heart) means relief.
Grund (ground) + Stück (piece). A piece of ground.\nEvery Grundstück is in the Grundbuch (land registry).

Grund (ground) + Stück (piece). A piece of ground. Property/plot of land. 'Ein Grundstück kaufen' (to buy a plot). German property law is thorough. Every Grundstück is in the Grundbuch (land registry).
'Den Rasen mähen' (to mow the lawn). 'Betreten des Rasens\nverboten' (Keep off the grass) is on signs in every German park.
'Den Rasen mähen' (to mow the lawn). 'Betreten des Rasens verboten' (Keep off the grass) is on signs in every German park. Germans take their Rasen seriously.
From 'flach' (flat). 'Grundfläche' (floor area),\n'Wasserfläche' (water surface). Measured in Quadratmetern.
From 'flach' (flat). 'Grundfläche' (floor area), 'Wasserfläche' (water surface). Measured in Quadratmetern. 'Die Fläche Deutschlands' is about 357,000 square kilometers.
From 'halb' (half). 'Zur Hälfte' (halfway).\nEnglish 'half' and German 'Hälfte' share the same Proto-Germanic root.
From 'halb' (half). 'Die Hälfte der Bevölkerung' (half the population). 'Zur Hälfte' (halfway). English 'half' and German 'Hälfte' share the same Proto-Germanic root.
From 'hoch' (high). 'Meereshöhe' (sea level). Also figuratively:\n'in voller Höhe' (in full amount).
From 'hoch' (high). 'In der Höhe' (at height). 'Meereshöhe' (sea level). Also figuratively: 'in voller Höhe' (in full amount). The Umlaut ö comes from the adjective hoch.
From 'lang' (long). 'In die Länge ziehen' (to drag out).\nAdjective to abstract noun via Umlaut: lang becomes Länge.
From 'lang' (long). 'In die Länge ziehen' (to drag out). 'Wellenlänge' (wavelength). Adjective to abstract noun via Umlaut: lang becomes Länge, just as hoch becomes Höhe.
An (at) + Zahl (number). More formal than 'Zahl' or 'Nummer.'\nUsed for countable quantities in statistics.
An (at) + Zahl (number). 'Die Anzahl der Teilnehmer' (the number of participants). More formal than 'Zahl' or 'Nummer.' Used for countable quantities in statistics and reports.
'Am Rand der Stadt' (on the edge of the city).\n'Außer Rand und Band' (out of control).
'Am Rand der Stadt' (on the edge of the city). 'Brillenrand' (frame of glasses). 'Außer Rand und Band' (out of control, literally out of edge and band).
Cognate with English 'shadow.' 'Im Schatten sitzen'\n(to sit in the shade). '30 Grad im Schatten' means it is really hot.
Cognate with English 'shadow' (from 'shade'). 'Im Schatten sitzen' (to sit in the shade). 'Schattenseite' (downside, literally shadow side). '30 Grad im Schatten' means it is really hot.
Ver- + packen (to pack). 'Umweltfreundlich verpackt.'\n'Verpackungsmüll' is a major environmental topic.
Ver- + packen (to pack). 'Umweltfreundlich verpackt' (packaged in an eco-friendly way). 'Verpackungsmüll' (packaging waste) is a major environmental topic. Germany has strict Verpackungsverordnung.
Ver- + brauchen (to need). 'Strom verbrauchen.' The ver-\nprefix means using something until it is gone.
Ver- + brauchen (to need). 'Strom verbrauchen' (to use electricity). 'Verbraucher' (consumer). The ver- prefix means using something until it is gone. Completely used up.
From 'Kunst' (art/artifice). 'Künstliches Licht' (artificial light).\n'Künstliche Intelligenz' (artificial intelligence).
From 'Kunst' (art/artifice). 'Künstliches Licht' (artificial light). 'Künstliche Intelligenz' (artificial intelligence). What Kunst creates is künstlich. Not natural, but made by human skill.
Um (around) + Gebung (giving/area). 'Die nahe Umgebung'\n(the immediate surroundings). What is given around you.
Um (around) + Gebung (giving/area). Surroundings. 'Die nahe Umgebung' (the immediate surroundings). 'In der Umgebung von Berlin' (in the Berlin area). What is given around you.
Wetter (weather) + Bericht (report). Germans check the\nWetterbericht religiously. Rain is always possible.
Wetter (weather) + Bericht (report). 'Den Wetterbericht lesen' (to read the weather report). Germans check the Wetterbericht religiously. Rain is always possible.
Wetter + Vorhersage (forecast). Longer and more formal\nthan Wetterbericht. Used in official contexts.
Wetter + Vorhersage (forecast). 'Laut Wettervorhersage regnet es morgen' (According to the forecast, it rains tomorrow). Longer and more formal than Wetterbericht.
Cognate with English 'storm.' 'Sturmwarnung' (storm warning).\nGermany's worst storms come from the North Sea.
Cognate with English 'storm.' 'Sturmwarnung' (storm warning). 'Im Sturm erobern' (to take by storm). Germany's worst storms come from the North Sea. Orkan = hurricane-force storm.
Related to the god Thor/Donar. 'Donnerstag' (Thursday) =\nThor's day. 'Donnerwetter!' is an exclamation of surprise.
Related to the god Thor/Donar. 'Donnerstag' (Thursday) = Thor's day. 'Donnerwetter!' is an exclamation of surprise (literally: thunder weather!). Both languages name Thursday after the thunder god.
From Italian 'all'arme' (to arms!). 'Feueralarm' (fire alarm),\n'Alarmstufe rot' (red alert). 'Alarm schlagen' (to sound the alarm).
From Italian 'all'arme' (to arms!). 'Feueralarm' (fire alarm), 'Alarmstufe rot' (red alert). 'Alarm schlagen' (to sound the alarm). International word with military origins.
From 'stören' (to disturb). Deutsche Bahn's favorite word:\n'Wegen einer Störung...' (Due to a disruption...).
From 'stören' (to disturb). 'Betriebsstörung' (service disruption). Deutsche Bahn's favorite word: 'Wegen einer Störung...' (Due to a disruption...). Every train delay starts here.
Unter (under) + stützen (to support). Support from below.\n'Um Unterstützung bitten' (to ask for support).
Unter (under) + stützen (to support). Support from below. 'Finanzielle Unterstützung' (financial support). 'Um Unterstützung bitten' (to ask for support). You hold something up from underneath.
Tier (animal) + Park. 'Der Berliner Tierpark' is the largest\nlandscape zoo in Europe. Emphasizes the park-like setting.
Tier (animal) + Park. 'Der Berliner Tierpark' is the largest landscape zoo in Europe. Different from 'Zoo' (which is also used). Tierpark emphasizes the park-like setting.
Be- + Schaden (damage). 'Der Sturm hat das Dach beschädigt.'\nRegular verb. The be- prefix makes 'Schaden' transitive.
Be- + Schaden (damage). 'Der Sturm hat das Dach beschädigt' (The storm damaged the roof). Regular verb. The be- prefix makes 'Schaden' into a transitive verb.
Irregular: brannte, gebrannt. Cognate with English 'burn.'\n'Es brennt!' (It's burning!) is what you shout during a fire.
Irregular: brannte, gebrannt. Cognate with English 'burn.' 'Es brennt!' (It is burning!) is what you shout during a fire. 'Brennholz' (firewood). 'Brennnessel' (stinging nettle, the burning nettle).
From Donner. 'Es donnert' (it is thundering).\nAlso: 'gegen die Tür donnern' (to bang on the door).
From Donner. 'Es donnert' (it is thundering). Also: 'gegen die Tür donnern' (to bang on the door). Regular despite its mythological origins. Thor would approve.
From 'Blitz' (lightning). 'Es blitzt' (there is lightning).\nSpeed cameras are called 'Blitzer' because they flash.
From 'Blitz' (lightning). 'Es blitzt' (there is lightning). 'Blitzschnell' (lightning fast). Also: speed cameras are called 'Blitzer' because they flash when they catch you.
From 'Hagel' (hail). 'Es hagelt' (it is hailing).\n'Es hagelt Beschwerden' (complaints are hailing down).
From 'Hagel' (hail). 'Es hagelt' (it is hailing). Hail can destroy cars, crops, and greenhouse roofs. 'Es hagelt Beschwerden' (complaints are hailing down) is used figuratively.
From 'trocken' (dry). 'Wäsche trocknen' (to dry laundry).\nGermans often air-dry laundry rather than using a dryer.
From 'trocken' (dry). 'Wäsche trocknen' (to dry laundry). Germans often air-dry laundry rather than using a Trockner (dryer). Energy-saving and laundry-friendly.
'Die Feuerwehr rettet Menschen.' 'Die Umwelt retten.'\n'Rettungswagen' (ambulance). A verb of heroic action.
'Die Feuerwehr rettet Menschen' (The fire department rescues people). 'Die Umwelt retten' (to save the environment). 'Rettungswagen' (ambulance). A verb of heroic action.
'Ein steiler Hügel' (a steep hill). 'Steile Karriere'\n(meteoric career). 'Steile These' (bold claim).
'Ein steiler Hügel' (a steep hill). 'Steile Karriere' (meteoric career). 'Steile These' (bold claim). From physical incline to figurative extremes.
Same word, same meaning as English. 'Wilde Tiere' (wild animals).\nGermany has more wild boars than any European country.
Same word, same meaning as English. 'Wilde Tiere' (wild animals), 'Wildnis' (wilderness). 'Wildschwein' (wild boar) roam Berlin's suburbs. Germany has more wild boars than any European country.
More colloquial than Schmutz. 'Im Dreck sitzen' (to be in trouble).\nNorthern German uses Dreck, southern prefers Schmutz.
More colloquial than Schmutz. 'Im Dreck sitzen' (to be in trouble, literally: sitting in dirt). 'Dreckig' (dirty/filthy). Northern German uses Dreck, southern prefers Schmutz.
More formal than Dreck. 'Schmutzig' (dirty).\n'Schmutzwasser' (dirty water).
More formal than Dreck. 'Schmutzig' (dirty). 'Schmutzwasser' (dirty water). The Sch- sound gives it a squelchy feel. German onomatopoeia at work.
Cognate with English 'dust' (via different path).\n'Staubsauger' (vacuum cleaner, literally dust-sucker).
Cognate with English 'dust' (via different path). 'Staubsauger' (vacuum cleaner, literally dust-sucker). 'Staub wischen' (to dust). 'Zu Staub werden' (to turn to dust).
Diminutive feel from the -el ending. 'Ameisenhügel' (anthill).\nGermany is full of Hügel in the Mittelgebirge region.
Diminutive feel from the -el ending, though not a true diminutive. 'Ameisenhügel' (anthill). Germany's landscape is full of Hügel, especially in the Mittelgebirge region.
Ge- + Berg (mountain) collective. 'Hochgebirge' (high mountains).\nThe Ge- prefix creates a collective: many mountains together.
Ge- + Berg (mountain) collective. Mountain range. 'Hochgebirge' (high mountains), 'Mittelgebirge' (medium-height mountains). The Ge- prefix creates a collective: many mountains together.
Cognate with English 'dale.' 'Rheintal' (Rhine valley).\n'Bergauf, bergab, durch jedes Tal.'
Cognate with English 'dale.' 'Rheintal' (Rhine valley), 'Moseltal' (Moselle valley). 'Bergauf, bergab, durch jedes Tal' (uphill, downhill, through every valley).
Cognate with English 'moon' and 'month' (Monat).\nSame root: time was measured by moons. 'Vollmond' (full moon).
Cognate with English 'moon' and 'month' (Monat). Same root: time was measured by moons. 'Vollmond' (full moon), 'Halbmond' (crescent/half moon). 'Mondschein' (moonshine/moonlight).
Cognate with English 'star' (via Latin 'stella').\n'Sternschnuppe' (shooting star). Also a famous German news magazine.
Cognate with English 'star' (via Latin 'stella'). 'Sternschnuppe' (shooting star, literally star-snip). 'Fünf-Sterne-Hotel' (five-star hotel). Stern is also a famous German news magazine.
From French 'alarme.' 'Lärmschutz' (noise protection).\nGermans have strict Ruhezeiten (quiet hours).
From French 'alarme.' 'Lärm machen' (to make noise). 'Lärmschutz' (noise protection). Germans have strict Ruhezeiten (quiet hours). Lawn mowing on Sunday? That is illegal Lärm.
Coined by Dutch chemist Van Helmont in the 1600s from Greek 'chaos.'\n'Erdgas' (natural gas). 'Gas geben' (to step on the gas).
Coined by Dutch chemist Van Helmont in the 1600s from Greek 'chaos.' 'Erdgas' (natural gas), 'Abgas' (exhaust gas). 'Gas geben' (to step on the gas/accelerate). A Dutch-invented word used worldwide.
Zer- + stören (to disturb). The zer- prefix means apart/to pieces.\nStronger than beschädigen (to damage). Total destruction.
Zer- + stören (to disturb). The zer- prefix means apart/to pieces. 'Die Umwelt zerstören' (to destroy the environment). Stronger than beschädigen (to damage). Total destruction.
Welten (worlds) + weit (wide). A poetic variant of weltweit.\n'Weltenweite Probleme.' The extra -en- adds a literary tone.
Welten (worlds) + weit (wide). A poetic variant of weltweit. Both are used. 'Weltenweite Probleme' (worldwide problems). The extra -en- adds a literary tone.
Welt + weit. The standard form. 'Weltweit bekannt'\n(known worldwide). More common than weltenweit.
Welt + weit. The standard form. 'Weltweit bekannt' (known worldwide). More common than weltenweit in everyday use. 'Weltweit einzigartig' (unique worldwide).
From Latin 'centralis.' 'Zentrale Lage' (central location).\n'Zentralheizung' (central heating).
From Latin 'centralis.' 'Zentrale Lage' (central location). 'Zentralheizung' (central heating). Every German apartment listing mentions whether it has Zentralheizung.
From Latin 'protestari' (to declare publicly). 'Klimaprotest.'\n'Fridays for Future' made Berlin a global protest capital.
From Latin 'protestari' (to declare publicly). 'Klimaprotest' (climate protest). 'Gegen etwas protestieren' (to protest against something). 'Fridays for Future' made Berlin a global protest capital.
From Latin 'alternare.' 'Alternativlos' was Germany's\nun-word of the year 2010. Means there is no other option.
From Latin 'alternare.' 'Eine Alternative finden' (to find an alternative). 'Alternativlos' was Germany's un-word of the year 2010. It means there is no other option.
Klima + Anlage (system/facility). Rare in German homes.\nMost Germans say: open the window. Fresh air beats everything.
Klima + Anlage (system/facility). Air conditioning. Rare in German homes. 'Brauchen wir eine Klimaanlage?' is the summer debate. Most Germans say no. Open the window.
Her (here/toward) + stellen (to place). The one who places\nsomething into existence. 'Vom Hersteller empfohlen.'

Her (here/toward) + stellen (to place). Manufacturer. 'Automobilhersteller' (car manufacturer). 'Vom Hersteller empfohlen' (recommended by the manufacturer). The one who places something into existence.
Feuer (fire) + Wehr (defense). Fire defense. Call 112.\nGerman volunteer fire departments have 1 million members.

Feuer (fire) + Wehr (defense). Fire defense = fire department. 'Die Feuerwehr rufen' (to call the fire department). Call 112. German volunteer fire departments have 1 million members. Heroes in every village.
Umwelt + Verschmutzung (pollution). 22 letters.\nA compound noun that carries the weight of the problem.
Umwelt + Verschmutzung (pollution). Environmental pollution. 22 letters. A compound noun that carries the weight of the problem. 'Umweltverschmutzung reduzieren' (to reduce environmental pollution).
From 'zünden' (to ignite). 'Zündstoff' (explosive material,\nalso figuratively: a hot topic). The dating app is named after this concept.
From 'zünden' (to ignite). 'Zündstoff' (explosive material, also figuratively: a hot topic). The dating app Tinder is named after this concept: something that ignites.
Zünd (ignite) + Holz (wood). Synonym of Streichholz.\nSouthern German prefers Zündholz, northern prefers Streichholz.
Zünd (ignite) + Holz (wood). A match. Synonym of Streichholz. Southern German prefers Zündholz, northern prefers Streichholz. Same object, regional word choice.
From Latin 'forma.' 'In Form' (in shape/fit).\n'Die Form wahren' (to keep up appearances).
From Latin 'forma.' 'In Form' (in shape/fit), 'Formular' (form to fill out). 'Die Form wahren' (to keep up appearances). A word that spans fitness, bureaucracy, and etiquette.
Unter (under) + stützen (to prop up). To support from below.\n'Die Regierung unterstützt erneuerbare Energien.'
Unter (under) + stützen (to prop up). To support from below. 'Erneuerbare Energien unterstützen' (to support renewable energies). Regular verb. Related to Unterstützung (support, the noun).
Ent- (away from) + sorgen (to care). To dispose of.\nThe ent- prefix removes the care: you send it away.
Ent- (away from) + sorgen (to care). To dispose of. 'Müll entsorgen' (to dispose of trash). 'Sondermüll entsorgen' (to dispose of hazardous waste). The ent- prefix removes the care: you send it away.
Takes dative. 'Der Umwelt schaden' (to harm the environment).\n'Rauchen schadet der Gesundheit.' Also a noun: der Schaden.
'Der Umwelt schaden' (to harm the environment). Takes dative. 'Rauchen schadet der Gesundheit' (Smoking damages health). Also a noun: der Schaden (the damage).
Ver- + groß (big). 'Das Bild vergrößern' (to enlarge the image).\nThe opposite: verkleinern (to make smaller).
Ver- + groß (big). 'Das Bild vergrößern' (to enlarge the image). 'Den Abstand vergrößern' (to increase the distance). The opposite: verkleinern (to make smaller).
Ver- + brennen. Irregular: verbrannte, verbrannt.\nThe ver- prefix means to completion: burned up entirely.
Ver- + brennen. Irregular: verbrannte, verbrannt. 'Müll verbrennen' (to incinerate trash). 'Sich verbrennen' (to burn oneself). The ver- prefix means to completion: burned up entirely.
From Greek 'elektron' (amber). 'Elektrisch' (electric),\n'Elektronik' (electronics), 'Elektrizität' (electricity).
From Greek 'elektron' (amber). 'Elektrisch' (electric), 'Elektronik' (electronics), 'Elektrizität' (electricity). The elektr- base forms adjectives and abstract nouns about electricity.
'Elektroauto' (electric car), 'Elektroherd' (electric stove).\nGermany's Elektromobilität push aims for 15 million e-cars by 2030.
'Elektroauto' (electric car), 'Elektroherd' (electric stove), 'Elektrogerät' (electrical device). Germany's Elektromobilität (e-mobility) push aims for 15 million e-cars by 2030.
From Greek 'oikos' (house/household). 'Ökologie' (ecology),\n'Ökostrom' (green electricity). 'Öko' by itself is slang for environmentalist.

From Greek 'oikos' (house/household). 'Ökologie' (ecology), 'Ökostrom' (green electricity), 'Ökosystem' (ecosystem). The prefix that turns everything green. 'Öko' by itself is slang for an environmentalist.
'Überdurchschnittlich' (above average), 'überall' (everywhere),\n'übertreiben' (to exaggerate). The most productive German prefix.
'Überdurchschnittlich' (above average), 'überall' (everywhere), 'übertreiben' (to exaggerate). The most productive German prefix. It intensifies everything it touches.

Germany's environmental awareness is deeply rooted.

Pfand: bottle deposit. 25 cents for plastic, 8 cents for glass. Return rate: 98%.
Mülltrennung: at least four bins. Neighbors will correct you.
Energiewende: the switch to renewable energy. Target: 80% by 2030.
Bio: Bio-Produkte hold large market shares. The Bio-Siegel is trusted.

When Mia says 'Du bist ein richtiger Berliner geworden,' she means more than geography. Separating waste, saving energy, returning bottles: these are acts of belonging.

Germany's relationship with nature has deep cultural roots. The Romantic movement (18th-19th century) celebrated forests and landscapes. The Wandervogel youth movement (1896) promoted hiking and outdoor life.

Today, this manifests as:
- The Green Party (Die Grünen): part of multiple federal governments
- Pfandsystem: 98% bottle return rate
- Energiewende: the most ambitious energy transition in the world
- Organic food market: largest in Europe

For immigrants, adopting environmental habits becomes a marker of cultural integration. When Mia says Verumius is a 'richtiger Berliner,' his environmental behavior is the proof.
